question: When the same moment comes back at night, which feels closest first?
your_response_prompt: Choose what feels most true right now
choices: I get pulled in quite a bit before noticing
I usually stay with it until the loop runs on
explanation: Mastery here is not "make it disappear forever." It is noticing a few seconds earlier so you have one more choice available.
actionable_advice: If you can say "it's starting again," that is already enough for the first step.

best_for: People who want to notice rumination earlier
limitations: When severe insomnia or low mood is ongoing
expanded_details.citation_role: Supports early noticing as a way to widen intervention room

question: You tell yourself, "I need to sort this out," then think for five minutes and keep circling the same conclusion. What is the closest view?
choices: This may be closer to a rumination loop than organizing
The longer I think, the more progress I make
If it hurts, I have to think it through to the end
explanation: In mastery, the question is not how much thinking happened. It is whether a new next step or new information appeared. If it keeps circling, it is closer to rumination.
actionable_advice: If no new next step appears, change mode before adding more thought.

best_for: People who want to tell organizing apart from rumination in real situations
limitations: Tasks that actually need outside information
expanded_details.citation_role: Supports the distinction between rumination and problem-solving thought

question: After getting home, you remember a mistake and your heart rate jumps. As a first move, it helps to label it as "my body is reacting" before digging into the content.
explanation: Right after noticing, settling the reaction can help more than pushing deeper into meaning. Treating it as a body response can create a bit more distance from the thought.
actionable_advice: Try one short label: "my body reacted first."

best_for: People whose stress response rises quickly
limitations: Situations where immediate safety comes first
expanded_details.citation_role: Supports labeling and distancing as an initial move
expanded_details.try_this: Label it for 10 seconds: My body reacted first.

tiny_metric.before_prompt: How pulled in do you feel right now? (0-10)
tiny_metric.success_rule: Any small distance from the thought is enough.
tiny_metric.stop_rule: If it gets stronger, stop digging into the content.

comparator.baseline: Going straight into finding the right answer in your head

fallback.next: Notice the feeling of your feet for 10 seconds

question: Choose all actions that fit right after rumination starts and you want to soften the loop a little.
choices: Count three things you can see before continuing the argument in your head
Keep replaying the scene until you find the perfect answer
Decide right away that it means something is wrong with your personality
explanation: The mastery target is not perfect analysis. It is slowing the speed of getting pulled in. A small attention shift or short self-talk can be the entrance to that.
actionable_advice: Pick one return move and reuse it every time rumination starts.

best_for: People who want to reduce the pull of rumination
expanded_details.citation_role: Supports attention shifting and short interventions as practical tools
expanded_details.try_this: Count three things you can see, then say: distance before conclusions.

tiny_metric.success_rule: Any reduction or one next action is enough.
tiny_metric.stop_rule: If stress rises, switch to another return move.

comparator.baseline: Continuing the argument in your head

question: Which one is closest to the mastery done condition?
choices: You notice rumination a little earlier and can choose one return move
You can always interpret every situation positively
explanation: Mastery aims for "notice earlier and return," not "make it zero forever." If you can come back even when it happens again, that is real learning value.

best_for: People who want a more realistic view of improvement
expanded_details.citation_role: Supports returning more easily rather than demanding zero recurrence
